sources.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology (Parker, Schneegurt, Tu, Lister, Forster)
sources.note: Multiple-choice Review Questions scraped verbatim; correctAnswer taken from OpenStax published Answer Key pages (pages/chapter-N). OpenStax posts a 'no LLM training' notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing per PRD, with attribution and non-commercial/share-alike compliance.

meta.notes: File 05: OpenStax Microbiology Ch. 5 (Eukaryotes of Microbiology) and Ch. 9 (Microbial Growth). All items are single-answer 4-option. Options 1-4 map to A-D; correctAnswer letters verified against the chapter answer keys.

questions.stem: Which genus includes the causative agent for malaria?
questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by apicomplexan parasites of the genus Plasmodium, transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: Malaria is spread by Anopheles mosquitoes and caused by which kind of organism?
hints.rationale: Malaria is caused by an apicomplexan protozoan parasite.

hints.prompt: Which listed genus is responsible for African sleeping sickness rather than malaria?
hints.rationale: Trypanosoma causes sleeping sickness, so it is not the malaria genus.

hints.prompt: Why is 'Euglena' not the cause of malaria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Euglena is a free-living, often photosynthetic flagellate, not a blood parasite

choices.label: B
choices.text: Euglena is the parasite injected by mosquitoes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Euglena invades red blood cells to cause malaria

hints.rationale: Euglena is a free-living protist, not the malaria-causing parasite.

questions.stem: Which protist is a concern because of its ability to contaminate water supplies and cause diarrheal illness?
questions.explanation: Giardia lamblia is a flagellated protist whose cysts contaminate water supplies and cause giardiasis, a diarrheal illness.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q2 = C.

hints.prompt: A protist spread through contaminated water and causing diarrhea infects which body system?
hints.rationale: A waterborne diarrheal protist infects the intestines.

hints.prompt: Plasmodium vivax and Toxoplasma gondii are known for which diseases (not waterborne diarrhea)?
hints.rationale: P. vivax causes malaria and T. gondii causes toxoplasmosis, so neither is the diarrheal water contaminant.

hints.prompt: Why is 'Trichomonas vaginalis' not the waterborne diarrheal pathogen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a sexually transmitted urogenital parasite, not a waterborne intestinal one

choices.label: B
choices.text: It contaminates drinking water and causes diarrhea

choices.label: C
choices.text: It forms cysts that spread through lakes

hints.rationale: Trichomonas vaginalis is a urogenital STI, not a water-contaminating diarrheal agent.

questions.stem: A fluke is classified within which of the following?
questions.explanation: Flukes (trematodes) are flatworms in the phylum Platyhelminthes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: A fluke (trematode) has which basic body plan?
hints.rationale: Flukes are flatworms.

hints.prompt: The phyla Nematoda and Annelida describe which worm types?
hints.rationale: Nematoda are roundworms and Annelida are segmented worms, neither being flatworms.

hints.prompt: Why is 'Annelida' not the phylum of a fluke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Annelids are segmented worms like earthworms, whereas flukes are unsegmented flatworms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Annelids are a kind of flatworm

hints.rationale: Annelids are segmented, unlike the flatworm body of a fluke.

questions.stem: A nonsegmented worm is found during a routine colonoscopy of an individual who reported having abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting. This worm is likely which of the following?
questions.explanation: Nematodes (roundworms) are nonsegmented worms; many are intestinal parasites. Flukes/trematodes are flatworms and annelids are segmented.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q4 = A.

hints.prompt: An unsegmented, cylindrical (round-bodied) worm is classified as a:
hints.rationale: A nonsegmented, round worm is a nematode.

hints.prompt: Are nematodes (roundworms) segmented or unsegmented?
hints.rationale: Nematodes are unsegmented, matching the described worm.

hints.prompt: Why can 'fluke' and 'trematode' both be ruled out for this worm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are two names for the same flatworm group, which is flattened rather than the round nematode form

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are both roundworms like the one described

choices.label: C
choices.text: One of them is a segmented worm

hints.rationale: Fluke and trematode are synonyms for flatworms, so neither fits a nonsegmented roundworm.

questions.stem: A segmented worm has male and female reproductive organs in each segment. Some use hooks to attach to the intestinal wall. Which type of worm is this?
questions.explanation: Cestodes (tapeworms) have segments (proglottids) each containing male and female reproductive organs and a scolex with hooks/suckers for attachment.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: A worm whose body is a chain of repeating segments, each with reproductive organs, is a:
hints.rationale: Tapeworms have repeating segments (proglottids) each bearing reproductive organs.

hints.prompt: Are nematodes (roundworms) built from repeating reproductive segments?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; each ring holds reproductive organs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; they use hooks on every segment

hints.rationale: Nematodes are unsegmented, so they do not fit the described segmented worm.

hints.prompt: Why is 'fluke' not the segmented worm described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flukes are unsegmented flatworms, lacking the repeating proglottid segments described

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flukes are segmented with organs in each segment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Flukes attach using a chain of segments

hints.rationale: Flukes are unsegmented, so they lack the segmentation described in the stem.

questions.stem: Mushrooms are a type of which of the following?
questions.explanation: A mushroom is a basidiocarp, the fruiting body of a basidiomycete fungus that bears spores on basidia.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q6 = D.

hints.prompt: A mushroom functions as which part of a fungus?
hints.rationale: A mushroom is a fungal fruiting body that bears spores.

hints.prompt: An 'ascus' is a spore-containing sac characteristic of which fungal group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ascomycetes (sac fungi), not the mushroom-forming group

hints.rationale: An ascus belongs to ascomycetes, so it is not what a mushroom is.

hints.prompt: Why is 'conidia' not what a mushroom is?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conidia are tiny asexual spores, not the large fruiting body that a mushroom represents

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mushroom is a single conidium

hints.rationale: Conidia are microscopic spores, whereas a mushroom is a macroscopic fruiting body.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common cause of human yeast infections?
questions.explanation: Candida albicans is the most common cause of human yeast infections (candidiasis), including oral, vaginal, and systemic forms.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q7 = A.

hints.prompt: Common human yeast infections such as thrush and vaginal candidiasis are caused by which genus?
hints.rationale: Candida species cause the most common human yeast infections.

hints.prompt: Aspergillus fumigatus is a mold that mainly causes what kind of illness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Respiratory (lung) infection, not ordinary yeast infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: The most common vaginal yeast infection

hints.rationale: Aspergillus causes respiratory aspergillosis, not the common yeast infections.

hints.prompt: Why is 'Cryptococcus neoformans' not the most common cause of ordinary yeast infections?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cryptococcus mainly causes meningitis in immunocompromised people, not everyday candidiasis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cryptococcus is the usual cause of thrush

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cryptococcus is not a fungus at all

hints.rationale: Cryptococcus typically causes meningitis, not the common yeast infections.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an ascomycete fungus associated with bat droppings that can cause a respiratory infection if inhaled?
questions.explanation: Histoplasma capsulatum grows in soil enriched by bat or bird droppings; inhaling its spores causes the respiratory infection histoplasmosis.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q8 = B.

hints.prompt: Inhaling fungal spores from soil enriched by bat or bird droppings can cause what kind of disease?
hints.rationale: Inhaled spores from such soil cause a respiratory fungal infection.

hints.prompt: Candida albicans is chiefly associated with which infections, making it a poor fit here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mucosal yeast infections (thrush, vaginal), not an inhaled soil fungus

hints.rationale: Candida causes mucosal yeast infections, unrelated to inhaled bat-dropping spores.

hints.prompt: Why is 'Trichophyton rubrum' not the bat-dropping respiratory fungus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Trichophyton causes skin infections such as athlete's foot, not an inhaled lung disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trichophyton is inhaled from bat caves

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trichophyton lives in bird droppings and infects lungs

hints.rationale: Trichophyton is a skin (dermatophyte) fungus, not a respiratory soil fungus.

questions.stem: Which polysaccharide found in red algal cell walls is a useful solidifying agent?
questions.explanation: Agar, a polysaccharide from red algal cell walls, is widely used as a solidifying agent for microbiological culture media.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: Which substance is widely used to solidify microbiological culture media?
hints.rationale: Agar is the standard gelling/solidifying agent for culture media.

hints.prompt: Chitin and cellulose are structural polysaccharides found in which structures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fungal cell walls (chitin) and plant cell walls (cellulose), not the red-algal solidifier

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red algal walls used to gel media

hints.rationale: Chitin and cellulose are wall polysaccharides of fungi and plants, not the media-gelling agent.

hints.prompt: Why is 'phycoerythrin' not the solidifying agent from red algae?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phycoerythrin is a red photosynthetic pigment, not a gelling polysaccharide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phycoerythrin is the main gelling agent in plates

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phycoerythrin is a cell-wall sugar used to solidify media

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phycoerythrin sets media at room temperature

hints.rationale: Phycoerythrin is a pigment, so it does not solidify media.

questions.stem: Which is the term for the hard outer covering of some dinoflagellates?
questions.explanation: Many dinoflagellates have a theca, an armor-like outer covering made of cellulose plates.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q10 = A.

hints.prompt: Some dinoflagellates are covered by protective plates made of cellulose. Such an armored covering is generally called a:
hints.rationale: The armor-like plated covering of dinoflagellates is the theca.

hints.prompt: The term 'mycelium' refers to a structure found in which organisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fungi (a mass of hyphae), not a dinoflagellate covering

hints.rationale: A mycelium is a fungal structure, not a dinoflagellate covering.

hints.prompt: Why is 'thallus' not the dinoflagellate armor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A thallus is an undifferentiated algal/fungal/lichen body, not a hard cellulose plate covering

choices.label: B
choices.text: A thallus is a single cellulose plate

choices.label: C
choices.text: A thallus is the dinoflagellate's shell

choices.label: D
choices.text: A thallus is a swimming flagellum

hints.rationale: A thallus is a whole body form, not the hard plated covering asked about.

questions.stem: Which protists are associated with red tides?
questions.explanation: Population blooms of certain dinoflagellates produce red tides and can release toxins that accumulate in shellfish.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q11 = C.

hints.prompt: Harmful blooms called red tides result from explosive population growth of which protists?
hints.rationale: Red tides are dinoflagellate blooms that can release toxins.

hints.prompt: Red, brown, and green 'algae' are named for their pigments; are these the red-tide organisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; red tides specifically involve dinoflagellate blooms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; brown algae cause red tides

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; green algae cause red tides

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes; all algae cause red tides equally

hints.rationale: Red tides are caused by dinoflagellates, not the pigment-named seaweed groups.

hints.prompt: Why is 'red algae' not the cause of red tides, despite the name?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red tides are dinoflagellate blooms; red algae are a separate group of multicellular seaweeds

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red algae are the only cause of red tides

choices.label: C
choices.text: Red algae are a type of dinoflagellate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Red algae release the red-tide toxins

hints.rationale: The name is misleading: red tides come from dinoflagellates, not red algae.

questions.stem: You encounter a lichen with leafy structures. Which term describes this lichen?
questions.explanation: Foliose lichens have flat, leaf-like lobes. Crustose lichens are flat and crusty, and fruticose lichens are shrubby/branched.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q12 = B.

hints.prompt: Lichen growth forms are named by shape. Which prefix relates to 'leaf'?
hints.rationale: A leafy lichen is foliose, from a root meaning leaf.

hints.prompt: A lichen that is flat and crusty, tightly stuck to a rock surface, is described as:
hints.rationale: A flat, crusty lichen is crustose, not the leafy form.

hints.prompt: Why is 'fruticose' not the term for a leafy lichen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fruticose lichens are shrubby and branched, not flat and leaf-like

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fruticose lichens are crusty and flat

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fruticose is a kind of alga

hints.rationale: Fruticose describes a shrubby, branching form, not a leafy one.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the term for the outer layer of a lichen?
questions.explanation: The cortex is the protective outer layer of a lichen; the medulla is the inner layer, and the thallus is the whole lichen body.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: In a layered structure, which term usually denotes the protective OUTER layer?
hints.rationale: The cortex is typically the outer protective layer.

hints.prompt: What is the inner layer of a lichen called?
hints.rationale: The medulla is the inner lichen layer, distinct from the outer layer.

hints.prompt: Why is 'thallus' not the outer layer of a lichen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The thallus is the entire lichen body, not specifically its outer layer

choices.label: B
choices.text: The thallus is only the outer skin

choices.label: C
choices.text: The thallus is the innermost layer

choices.label: D
choices.text: The thallus is a fungal spore

hints.rationale: The thallus refers to the whole lichen body rather than one layer.

questions.stem: The fungus in a lichen is which of the following?
questions.explanation: In most lichens the fungal partner (mycobiont) is an ascomycete, living in symbiosis with an alga or cyanobacterium.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 5 Eukaryotes of Microbiology — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-5), Ch.5 MC Q14 = B.

hints.prompt: A lichen is a symbiosis between a fungus and which kind of partner?
hints.rationale: A lichen pairs a fungus with a photosynthetic alga or cyanobacterium.

hints.prompt: An 'apicomplexan' is a type of what, which makes it impossible as the lichen's fungal partner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A protozoan parasite (not a fungus at all)

hints.rationale: An apicomplexan is a protozoan parasite, so it cannot be the fungal partner.

hints.prompt: Why is 'a zygomycete' not the usual lichen fungus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most lichen fungi are ascomycetes (sac fungi), not zygomycetes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Zygomycetes are the standard lichen partner

hints.rationale: The typical lichen mycobiont is an ascomycete, not a zygomycete.

questions.stem: Which of the following methods would be used to measure the concentration of bacterial contamination in processed peanut butter?

choices.label: D
choices.text: direct counting of bacteria on a calibrated slide under the microscope

questions.explanation: A total (viable) plate count is suited to measuring low levels of viable bacteria in an opaque solid food like peanut butter, where turbidity and dry-weight methods would not work.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: To count a small number of LIVE bacteria in an opaque solid food, the best method is one that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Grows colonies from viable cells and counts them (a viable plate count)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measures the cloudiness of a clear liquid

hints.rationale: A viable plate count grows and counts colonies from living cells.

hints.prompt: Why can turbidity (cloudiness) measurement not be used on peanut butter?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Turbidity requires a clear liquid suspension, but peanut butter is opaque and solid

hints.rationale: Turbidity needs a clear liquid, so it fails on an opaque solid food.

hints.prompt: Why is 'dry weight measurement' a poor choice here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dry weight estimates large amounts of biomass and cannot separate low-level microbes from the food

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dry weight precisely counts only living cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dry weight requires a perfectly clear solution

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dry weight is identical to a viable plate count

hints.rationale: Dry weight suits large biomass, not low-level viable contamination in food.

questions.stem: In which phase would you expect to observe the most endospores in a Bacillus cell culture?

choices.label: D
choices.text: log, lag, and death phases would all have roughly the same number of endospores.

questions.explanation: Endospore formation is triggered by nutrient depletion and stress, which are greatest in the death phase, so the most endospores are seen then.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q2 = A.

hints.prompt: Bacterial endospore formation is triggered mainly by:

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fastest rate of cell division

hints.rationale: Endospores form in response to starvation and stress.

hints.prompt: During the log (exponential) phase, nutrients are plentiful and cells divide rapidly. Is this when spores form?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; sporulation is triggered by starvation, not by abundant growth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; log phase produces the most spores

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes; spores only form during fast growth

hints.rationale: Log phase is nutrient-rich, so it does not trigger the most sporulation.

hints.prompt: Why is 'lag phase' not when the most endospores appear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In lag phase cells are adjusting with nutrients still available, so sporulation is not yet triggered

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lag phase is the most nutrient-starved phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lag phase has the greatest cell death

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lag phase always follows the death phase

hints.rationale: Nutrients are still available in lag phase, so few spores form then.

questions.stem: During which phase would penicillin, an antibiotic that inhibits cell-wall synthesis, be most effective?
questions.explanation: Penicillin targets actively dividing cells building new cell wall, so it is most effective during the log (exponential) phase of rapid growth.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: Penicillin blocks new cell-wall synthesis, so it harms bacteria that are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Actively dividing and building new cell wall

hints.rationale: Penicillin targets cells actively building new cell wall.

hints.prompt: During the stationary and death phases, is net cell-wall synthesis high or low?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low, because there is little net growth

choices.label: C
choices.text: The same as during rapid growth

hints.rationale: Little wall is being built in stationary/death phases, so penicillin is less effective then.

hints.prompt: Why is 'lag phase' not when penicillin is most effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In lag phase cells are adapting and not yet dividing rapidly, so little new wall is being built

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lag phase has the fastest wall synthesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Penicillin cannot act during any growth

hints.rationale: Cells are not yet dividing quickly in lag phase, so wall synthesis is minimal.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best definition of generation time in a bacterium?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the length of time it takes to reach the log phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: the length of time it takes for a population of cells to double

choices.label: C
choices.text: the time it takes to reach stationary phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: the length of time of the exponential phase

questions.explanation: Generation (doubling) time is the time required for a bacterial population to double in number.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q4 = B.

hints.prompt: During exponential growth, a bacterial population increases by what pattern?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adding a fixed number of cells each hour

hints.rationale: Bacteria grow by doubling via binary fission.

hints.prompt: Does 'generation time' refer to how long the entire exponential phase lasts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it is the interval for one doubling, not the length of a growth phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; it equals the whole log phase duration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; it is the time in stationary phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes; it is the death-phase duration

hints.rationale: Generation time is a doubling interval, not the duration of a phase.

hints.prompt: Why is 'the length of time to reach the log phase' incorrect for generation time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That describes the lag before exponential growth, not the population-doubling interval

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is exactly the doubling time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Generation time means time to reach stationary phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the time cells spend dead

hints.rationale: Time to reach log phase is the lag, not the doubling (generation) time.

questions.stem: What is the function of the Z ring in binary fission?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It controls the replication of DNA.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It forms a contractile ring at the septum.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It separates the newly synthesized DNA molecules.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It mediates the addition of new peptidoglycan subunits.

questions.explanation: The Z ring, made of FtsZ protein, assembles at the midcell septum and forms a contractile ring that drives division during binary fission.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: For a bacterium to split into two cells, what must happen at its midpoint?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It must constrict (pinch inward) at the midcell division site

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must double in width without dividing

hints.rationale: Division requires constriction at the midcell site.

hints.prompt: Where does the FtsZ Z ring assemble?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At the midcell septum (the division plane)

hints.rationale: The Z ring forms at the midcell septum where division occurs.

hints.prompt: Why is 'It controls the replication of DNA' wrong for the Z ring?

choices.label: A
choices.text: DNA replication is done by DNA polymerase and origin machinery, not the FtsZ Z ring

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Z ring is the cell's main DNA polymerase

choices.label: C
choices.text: The Z ring copies the chromosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The Z ring pulls the two DNA copies apart

hints.rationale: The Z ring drives division, whereas DNA replication is a separate process.

questions.stem: If a culture starts with 50 cells, how many cells will be present after five generations with no cell death?
questions.explanation: Each generation doubles the population: 50 x 2^5 = 50 x 32 = 1600 cells.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q6 = C.

hints.prompt: After each generation of binary fission, the number of cells is multiplied by:
hints.rationale: Each generation doubles the population (multiply by 2).

hints.prompt: Over 5 generations, the starting number is multiplied by 2 raised to what power?
hints.rationale: Five doublings multiply the start by 2^5 = 32.

hints.prompt: Why is '200' the result of a wrong calculation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 200 comes from multiplying 50 by 4 (only 2 doublings) instead of by 32 (five doublings)

hints.rationale: 200 reflects too few doublings, not five full generations.

questions.stem: Filamentous cyanobacteria often divide by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Filamentous cyanobacteria commonly reproduce by fragmentation, in which pieces of the filament break off and grow into new filaments.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: A long filamentous organism can reproduce by breaking into pieces that each grow into a new filament. This is called:
hints.rationale: Breaking into growing pieces is fragmentation.

hints.prompt: Do bacteria, including cyanobacteria, reproduce by mitosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; mitosis is a eukaryotic process

hints.rationale: Mitosis is eukaryotic, so it is not how cyanobacteria divide.

hints.prompt: Why is 'formation of endospores' not the usual way filamentous cyanobacteria multiply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endospores are dormant survival structures, not a routine means of reproducing the filament

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endospores are how filaments normally split during growth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endospores are the same thing as fragments

hints.rationale: Endospores are for survival, not the normal reproduction of a filament.

questions.stem: Which is a reason for antimicrobial resistance being higher in a biofilm than in free-floating bacterial cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The EPS allows faster diffusion of chemicals in the biofilm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cells are more metabolically active at the base of a biofilm.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cells are metabolically inactive at the base of a biofilm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The structure of a biofilm favors the survival of antibiotic resistant cells.

questions.explanation: Many antimicrobials target active processes; cells deep in a biofilm are metabolically inactive (dormant), making them less susceptible and increasing resistance.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q8 = C.

hints.prompt: Many antibiotics act on active cellular processes, so which cells are LEAST affected?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cells building lots of new wall

hints.rationale: Dormant cells are least vulnerable to drugs that target active processes.

hints.prompt: Does the biofilm's extracellular matrix (EPS) speed up or slow the diffusion of antimicrobials?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It slows/hinders diffusion of many chemicals

choices.label: B
choices.text: It speeds diffusion so drugs act faster

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has no effect on diffusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: It only affects nutrients, never drugs

hints.rationale: The EPS hinders diffusion, contradicting the claim that it speeds it up.

hints.prompt: Why is 'cells are more metabolically active at the base of a biofilm' incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cells deep at the base face limited nutrients and oxygen, so they are actually less active

choices.label: B
choices.text: The base has the most nutrients and oxygen

choices.label: C
choices.text: The base is the most metabolically active zone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Metabolic activity is identical throughout the biofilm

hints.rationale: Nutrient/oxygen limits make basal cells less active, not more.

questions.stem: Quorum sensing is used by bacterial cells to determine which of the following?
questions.explanation: Quorum sensing lets bacteria gauge population density by detecting the concentration of secreted autoinducers, coordinating group behaviors once a threshold is reached.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: Bacteria secrete autoinducers whose concentration rises as more cells accumulate. Detecting that concentration tells them about:

choices.label: A
choices.text: How crowded the local population is

hints.rationale: Autoinducer concentration reports how crowded the population is.

hints.prompt: Does quorum sensing directly measure nutrient availability?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it senses autoinducer concentration, reflecting crowding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; it measures nutrient levels directly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes; it counts nearby nutrients only

hints.rationale: Quorum sensing gauges autoinducer concentration, not nutrients directly.

hints.prompt: Why is 'the size of the population' a weaker answer than population density?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoinducers report concentration (cells per volume, i.e., density), not an absolute head count independent of space

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autoinducers count total cells regardless of volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quorum sensing ignores cell numbers entirely

hints.rationale: Autoinducer signals reflect density (concentration), which is not the same as raw population size.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about autoinducers is incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They bind directly to DNA to activate transcription.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They can activate the cell that secreted them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: N-acylated homoserine lactones are autoinducers in gram-negative cells.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autoinducers may stimulate the production of virulence factors.

questions.explanation: Autoinducers act through receptor proteins/signal pathways rather than binding DNA directly. The other statements are true.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q10 = A.

hints.prompt: Signaling molecules such as autoinducers typically act by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Binding receptor proteins that then change gene expression

hints.rationale: Autoinducers work through receptor proteins, not by binding DNA directly.

hints.prompt: Are the statements 'they can activate the secreting cell' and 'AHLs are gram-negative autoinducers' true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, both are accurate statements about autoinducers

hints.rationale: Both statements are true, so neither is the 'incorrect' one.

hints.prompt: Since the question asks for the INCORRECT statement, why is 'autoinducers may stimulate virulence factors' not the answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That statement is true, so it cannot be the incorrect one

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is false because autoinducers never affect virulence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Virulence factors have nothing to do with bacteria

choices.label: D
choices.text: It means the same thing as binding DNA

hints.rationale: Autoinducers do regulate virulence factors, so this true statement is not the exception.

questions.stem: An inoculated thioglycolate medium culture tube shows dense growth at the surface and turbidity throughout the rest of the tube. What is your conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The organisms die in the presence of oxygen

choices.label: C
choices.text: The organisms should be grown in an anaerobic chamber.

questions.explanation: Growth densest at the oxygen-rich surface but present throughout indicates facultative anaerobes, which grow best with oxygen but can also grow without it.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: In a thioglycolate tube, oxygen is highest at the top and lowest at the bottom, so where a microbe grows reveals its:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relationship to oxygen (its oxygen requirement or tolerance)

hints.rationale: Growth position in the oxygen gradient shows the organism's oxygen relationship.

hints.prompt: If an organism grew ONLY at the very top of the tube, it would be:
hints.rationale: Growth only at the top marks an obligate aerobe; growth throughout is a different pattern.

hints.prompt: Why does 'the organisms die in the presence of oxygen' contradict this result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They grew densely at the oxygen-rich surface, so oxygen is clearly not lethal to them

choices.label: B
choices.text: They showed no growth at the surface

choices.label: D
choices.text: They grew only at the bottom

hints.rationale: Dense surface growth shows oxygen is tolerated, ruling out oxygen being lethal.

questions.stem: An inoculated thioglycolate medium culture tube is clear throughout the tube except for dense growth at the bottom of the tube. What is your conclusion?
questions.explanation: Growth only at the oxygen-poor bottom indicates obligate anaerobes, which cannot grow where oxygen is present.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q12 = A.

hints.prompt: In a thioglycolate tube, growth confined to the oxygen-poor bottom indicates the organism:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Grows only where oxygen is absent

choices.label: C
choices.text: Grows best at the oxygen-rich surface

hints.rationale: Bottom-only growth means the organism grows where oxygen is absent.

hints.prompt: Would an obligate aerobe grow at the oxygen-poor bottom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; an obligate aerobe needs oxygen and would grow near the top

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; obligate aerobes prefer the bottom

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; oxygen is highest at the bottom

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if the tube is shaken

hints.rationale: Obligate aerobes need oxygen, so bottom-only growth is not theirs.

hints.prompt: Why is 'aerotolerant' not the best fit for growth ONLY at the bottom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aerotolerant anaerobes grow evenly throughout the tube because they tolerate oxygen, not just at the bottom

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aerotolerant organisms grow only at the surface

hints.rationale: Aerotolerant organisms grow throughout, unlike the bottom-only pattern shown.

questions.stem: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a common pathogen that infects the airways of patients with cystic fibrosis. It does not grow in the absence of oxygen. The bacterium is probably which of the following?
questions.explanation: An organism that requires oxygen and cannot grow without it is an obligate aerobe.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: The terms aerobe and anaerobe classify microbes by their relationship to which gas?
hints.rationale: Aerobe/anaerobe classifications are based on oxygen.

hints.prompt: Since P. aeruginosa does not grow without oxygen, oxygen is for it:
hints.rationale: Being unable to grow without oxygen means oxygen is required.

hints.prompt: Why is 'facultative anaerobe' incorrect for P. aeruginosa here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A facultative anaerobe can grow without oxygen, but this organism cannot

choices.label: C
choices.text: Facultative anaerobes are killed by oxygen

hints.rationale: Facultative anaerobes grow with or without oxygen, unlike this strictly oxygen-dependent organism.

questions.stem: Streptococcus mutans is a major cause of cavities. It resides in the gum pockets, does not have catalase activity, and can be grown outside of an anaerobic chamber. The bacterium is probably which of the following?
questions.explanation: An organism that does not use oxygen (and lacks catalase) yet tolerates its presence and grows outside an anaerobic chamber is an aerotolerant anaerobe.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q14 = D.

hints.prompt: Being able to grow in open air (outside an anaerobic chamber) shows an organism can at least do what with oxygen?
hints.rationale: Growing in air shows the organism can tolerate oxygen.

hints.prompt: Does S. mutans, which does not use oxygen for energy, require oxygen (making it an obligate aerobe)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it does not use oxygen, so it is not an obligate aerobe

hints.rationale: S. mutans does not use oxygen, so it is not an obligate aerobe.

hints.prompt: Why is 'obligate anaerobe' incorrect for S. mutans?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obligate anaerobes are harmed by oxygen and cannot grow in air, yet S. mutans grows outside an anaerobic chamber

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obligate anaerobes thrive in open air

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obligate anaerobes are full of catalase

hints.rationale: Because S. mutans grows in air, it is not an oxygen-intolerant obligate anaerobe.

questions.stem: Why do the instructions for the growth of Neisseria gonorrhoeae recommend a CO2-enriched atmosphere?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses CO2 as a final electron acceptor in respiration.

questions.explanation: Capnophiles such as Neisseria gonorrhoeae require an elevated CO2 concentration for optimal growth.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q15 = C.

hints.prompt: A microbe that grows best with an elevated concentration of carbon dioxide is called a:
hints.rationale: An organism needing high CO2 is a capnophile.

hints.prompt: The prefix 'capno-' refers to a requirement for which gas?
hints.rationale: 'Capno-' relates to carbon dioxide, matching a capnophile's need.

hints.prompt: Why is 'It uses CO2 as a final electron acceptor in respiration' the wrong reason for needing CO2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Requiring elevated CO2 for growth (capnophily) is not the same as using CO2 as a respiratory electron acceptor

choices.label: B
choices.text: All capnophiles respire using CO2 as the electron acceptor

choices.label: C
choices.text: CO2 is the universal electron acceptor for bacteria

choices.label: D
choices.text: It proves the organism is a strict anaerobe

hints.rationale: Capnophily is a growth requirement for CO2, distinct from using CO2 in respiration.

questions.stem: Bacteria that grow in mine drainage at pH 1–2 are probably which of the following?
questions.explanation: Organisms that grow optimally at very low pH (1–2) are acidophiles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q16 = B.

hints.prompt: Microbes that grow best at very low (acidic) pH are called:
hints.rationale: Acid-loving organisms are acidophiles.

hints.prompt: Is a pH of 1 to 2 strongly acidic or strongly basic?
hints.rationale: pH 1-2 is strongly acidic, favoring acidophiles.

hints.prompt: Why is 'alkaliphiles' incorrect for organisms growing at pH 1 to 2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alkaliphiles prefer high (basic) pH, the opposite of acidic pH 1-2

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alkaliphiles grow only at neutral pH

hints.rationale: Alkaliphiles favor basic pH, so they do not fit an acidic pH 1-2 habitat.

questions.stem: Bacteria isolated from Lake Natron, where the water pH is close to 10, are which of the following?
questions.explanation: Organisms that thrive at high (basic) pH near 10 are alkaliphiles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q17 = A.

hints.prompt: Microbes adapted to grow at high (basic) pH are called:
hints.rationale: Base-loving organisms are alkaliphiles.

hints.prompt: Is a water pH close to 10 acidic, neutral, or basic?
hints.rationale: pH 10 is basic, favoring alkaliphiles.

hints.prompt: Why is 'neutrophiles' incorrect for water at pH 10?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neutrophiles prefer near-neutral pH around 7, not the basic pH 10

hints.rationale: Neutrophiles favor pH near 7, so pH 10 is outside their optimum.

questions.stem: In which environment are you most likely to encounter an acidophile?
questions.explanation: Acidophiles favor very low pH, so a hot vent at pH 1.5 is the most likely habitat.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q18 = B.

hints.prompt: An acidophile would be found in an environment that is:
hints.rationale: Acidophiles inhabit strongly acidic (low-pH) environments.

hints.prompt: Is human blood at pH 7.2 acidic or near-neutral?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Near-neutral, so it would not host an acidophile

hints.rationale: Blood at pH 7.2 is near-neutral, unsuitable for an acidophile.

hints.prompt: Why is 'human intestine at pH 8.5' not where you would find an acidophile?

choices.label: A
choices.text: pH 8.5 is basic, which is unsuitable for an acid-loving organism

choices.label: C
choices.text: The intestine is the most acidic site listed

hints.rationale: pH 8.5 is basic, so it does not suit an acidophile.

questions.stem: A soup container was forgotten in the refrigerator and shows contamination. The contaminants are probably which of the following?
questions.explanation: Microbes that grow at refrigeration temperatures (but can also grow warmer) are psychrotrophs, a common cause of refrigerated-food spoilage.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q19 = D.

hints.prompt: Microbes able to grow at cold refrigerator temperatures are generally:
hints.rationale: Refrigerator spoilage organisms tolerate cold (psychrotrophic).

hints.prompt: Refrigeration slows spoilage, so organisms that still grow there must tolerate what?
hints.rationale: Growing in a fridge requires cold tolerance.

hints.prompt: Why are 'thermophiles' not the likely refrigerator contaminants?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thermophiles require high temperatures and would not grow in a cold refrigerator

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thermophiles cannot grow above 20 degrees C

hints.rationale: Thermophiles need heat, so they will not grow in the cold.

questions.stem: Bacteria isolated from a hot tub at 39 °C are probably which of the following?
questions.explanation: An optimal growth temperature near 39 °C (close to body temperature) is characteristic of mesophiles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q20 = C.

hints.prompt: Microbes that grow best at moderate temperatures (roughly 20-45 degrees C, including body temperature) are:
hints.rationale: Moderate-temperature organisms are mesophiles.

hints.prompt: About 39 degrees C is closest to which familiar temperature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Human body temperature, a moderate temperature

hints.rationale: 39 degrees C is near body temperature, a moderate range.

hints.prompt: Why are 'thermophiles' not the best answer for 39 degrees C?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thermophiles are adapted to much hotter conditions (roughly 50-70 degrees C), well above 39

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thermophiles grow best right at 39 degrees C

choices.label: C
choices.text: 39 degrees C is extremely hot for any microbe

hints.rationale: Thermophiles need higher heat, so 39 degrees C favors mesophiles instead.

questions.stem: In which environment are you most likely to encounter a hyperthermophile?

choices.label: C
choices.text: hydrothermal vent at the bottom of the ocean

questions.explanation: Hyperthermophiles grow at extremely high temperatures, such as those found at deep-sea hydrothermal vents.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q21 = C.

hints.prompt: A hyperthermophile requires an environment that is:
hints.rationale: Hyperthermophiles grow at extremely high temperatures.

hints.prompt: Is the human body (about 37 degrees C) hot enough for a hyperthermophile?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it is far too cool for a hyperthermophile

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; body temperature is ideal for them

hints.rationale: Body temperature is far below a hyperthermophile's requirement.

hints.prompt: Why is 'warm ocean water in Florida' not suitable for a hyperthermophile?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warm surface seawater is far cooler than the extreme heat hyperthermophiles require

choices.label: B
choices.text: Florida seawater regularly exceeds 80 degrees C

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the hottest environment listed

hints.rationale: Surface seawater is far too cool for a hyperthermophile.

questions.stem: Which of the following environments would harbor psychrophiles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: mountain lake with a water temperature of 12 °C

choices.label: B
choices.text: contaminated plates left in a 35 °C incubator

choices.label: D
choices.text: salt pond in the desert with a daytime temperature of 34 °C

questions.explanation: Psychrophiles grow best at cold temperatures, so a cold mountain lake at 12 °C is the most suitable of these environments.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q22 = A.

hints.prompt: Psychrophiles are adapted to grow best at:
hints.rationale: Psychrophiles thrive in the cold.

hints.prompt: Would a 35 degrees C incubator or room-temperature yogurt culture favor a cold-loving organism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; both are too warm for a psychrophile

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; 35 degrees C is ideal for psychrophiles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; room temperature is freezing to them

hints.rationale: Both settings are warm, unsuitable for cold-loving psychrophiles.

hints.prompt: Why is 'salt pond in the desert at 34 degrees C' unsuitable for psychrophiles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 34 degrees C is warm, not the cold environment psychrophiles require

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deserts are the coldest habitats on Earth

hints.rationale: A 34 degrees C pond is warm, so it does not suit cold-loving organisms.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the reason jams and dried meats often do not require refrigeration to prevent spoilage?
questions.explanation: High sugar or salt and drying reduce water activity, limiting the water microbes need to grow, which preserves jams and dried meats.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q23 = D.

hints.prompt: Adding lots of sugar or salt, or drying a food, preserves it mainly by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lowering the water available for microbial growth

hints.rationale: These methods reduce available water (water activity) that microbes need.

hints.prompt: Microbes need available water to grow; a high-sugar jam or dried meat provides how much?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The same water as fresh food

hints.rationale: High-sugar or dried foods have low available water, limiting microbial growth.

hints.prompt: Why is 'naturally occurring antibiotics' not why jams resist spoilage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Jams and dried meats are preserved by low water availability, not by containing antibiotics

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fruit jam is naturally full of antibiotics

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibiotics are the main preservative in dried meat

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sugar itself acts as an antibiotic

hints.rationale: Preservation here comes from low water activity, not antibiotics.

questions.stem: Bacteria living in salt marshes are most likely which of the following?
questions.explanation: Organisms that can grow in the elevated salinity of salt marshes are halotolerant (able to tolerate high salt).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q24 = C.

hints.prompt: Microbes able to grow in elevated salt concentrations are described as:
hints.rationale: Organisms that grow in high salt are halotolerant.

hints.prompt: Salt marshes are characterized by elevated levels of what?
hints.rationale: Salt marshes have high salinity, selecting for salt tolerance.

hints.prompt: Why is 'barophiles' not the best answer for salt-marsh bacteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Barophiles are adapted to high pressure (such as the deep sea), not high salt

choices.label: C
choices.text: Salt marshes are extreme high-pressure habitats

choices.label: D
choices.text: Barophile is another word for salt-tolerant

hints.rationale: Barophiles favor high pressure, whereas salt marshes select for salt tolerance.

questions.stem: EMB agar is a medium used in the identification and isolation of pathogenic bacteria. It contains digested meat proteins as a source of organic nutrients. Two indicator dyes, eosin and methylene blue, inhibit the growth of gram-positive bacteria and distinguish between lactose fermenting and nonlactose fermenting organisms. Lactose fermenters form metallic green or deep purple colonies, whereas the nonlactose fermenters form completely colorless colonies. EMB agar is an example of which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: a selective medium and a chemically defined medium

choices.label: D
choices.text: a selective medium, a differential medium, and a complex medium

questions.explanation: EMB agar selects against gram-positives (selective), distinguishes lactose fermenters by colony color (differential), and uses digested proteins of unknown exact composition (complex), so it is all three.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q25 = D.

hints.prompt: A culture medium that inhibits the growth of some organisms while permitting others is called:
hints.rationale: A medium that suppresses some organisms is selective.

hints.prompt: EMB also makes lactose fermenters form colored colonies. A medium that visibly distinguishes organisms is:
hints.rationale: Distinguishing organisms by appearance makes a medium differential.

hints.prompt: Why is 'a differential medium only' an incomplete description of EMB?

choices.label: A
choices.text: EMB also selectively inhibits gram-positive bacteria, so it is more than differential alone

choices.label: B
choices.text: EMB does not distinguish colonies at all

choices.label: C
choices.text: EMB inhibits nothing, so it cannot be selective

choices.label: D
choices.text: EMB has a completely defined chemical formula

hints.rationale: EMB is both selective and differential (and complex), so 'differential only' understates it.

questions.stem: Haemophilus influenzae must be grown on chocolate agar, which is blood agar treated with heat to release growth factors in the medium. H. influenzae is described as ________.
questions.explanation: An organism with complex, specific nutritional requirements (such as the growth factors released into chocolate agar) is described as fastidious.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 9 Microbial Growth — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-9), Ch.9 MC Q26 = D.

hints.prompt: A microbe that requires special, complex nutrients in order to grow is described as:
hints.rationale: An organism with demanding nutrient needs is fastidious.

hints.prompt: Needing growth factors specially released into chocolate agar reflects what kind of requirement?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A need for very low pH

choices.label: D
choices.text: A need to avoid all oxygen

hints.rationale: Requiring released growth factors is a nutritional demand, i.e., fastidiousness.

hints.prompt: Why does needing chocolate agar's growth factors NOT indicate 'an obligate anaerobe'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Requiring specific nutrients (fastidious) concerns nutrition, not the ability to tolerate oxygen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chocolate agar proves the organism cannot tolerate oxygen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fastidious is another word for obligate anaerobe

hints.rationale: The chocolate-agar requirement is about nutrition, unrelated to oxygen tolerance.
